items.q: What is the difference between mass and weight?
items.correct: Mass is the amount of matter (kg); weight is the gravitational force on that mass (N) and depends on g.
items.misconception: Mass and weight are the same thing, just expressed in different units.
items.random_wrong: Mass changes with location but weight does not.
items.why: Students confuse mass with weight because everyday English uses 'weight' for both, and bathroom scales display mass in kg labelled as 'weight'.

items.q: If air resistance is ignored, which falls faster: a heavy ball or a light ball dropped from the same height?
items.correct: Both fall at the same rate because gravitational acceleration is independent of mass.
items.misconception: Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects because gravity pulls more on them.
items.random_wrong: The lighter ball falls faster because it has less inertia.
items.why: Students reason 'more gravity force = more acceleration' but forget the heavier object also has proportionally more inertia, cancelling out.

items.q: When a wave travels through water, what happens to the water itself?
items.correct: The water oscillates about a fixed point; the wave transfers energy, not matter.
items.misconception: The water moves along with the wave from one place to another.
items.random_wrong: The water turns into wave material and changes state.
items.why: Visually waves look like 'water moving across the surface', so students think water itself travels with the wave, missing that only energy is transferred.
Correct answer: increasing time of collision. “increasing mass” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: cosmic microwave background. “cosmic mass body” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Crumple zone doubles collision time. Force is multiplied by:
items.why: Exact answer: 0.5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
